attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of bright red rectal bleeding and urgency to open his bowels. He reports passing small amounts of blood with bowel movements and occasional mucus. He has lost 2 kg in weight but maintains a good appetite. His medical history includes prostate cancer treated with radiotherapy two years ago. He takes no regular medications. He has never had a colonoscopy.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Digital rectal examination reveals no masses but there is bright red blood on the glove. His haemoglobin is 118 g/L (130-170).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with rectal bleeding and tenesmus two years after pelvic radiotherapy for prostate cancer. The timing and symptoms are classic for radiation proctitis, which typically develops months to years after radiation exposure to the rectum. The condition occurs in up to 20% of patients receiving pelvic radiotherapy.

Colorectal cancer remains a possibility given the patient's age and weight loss. However, the temporal relationship with radiotherapy and the nature of symptoms make radiation proctitis more likely. Nevertheless, colonoscopy would still be warranted to exclude malignancy.

Inflammatory bowel disease typically presents at a younger age and would be unusual to develop de novo in a 72-year-old. Additionally, patients usually have systemic symptoms such as fatigue, joint pains, or skin manifestations.

Haemorrhoids commonly cause bright red rectal bleeding but typically the blood is seen on the toilet paper or drips into the bowl. They would not explain the urgency and tenesmus described here.

Diverticular disease more commonly causes left-sided abdominal pain and altered bowel habits. While it can cause rectal bleeding, this is typically painless and self-limiting.

Radiation proctitis occurs due to radiation-induced damage to the rectal mucosa, leading to telangiectasia and ulceration. Diagnosis is confirmed by colonoscopy, which shows characteristic telangiectasia in the rectum confined to the radiation field. Treatment options include topical sucralfate, oral metronidazole, or endoscopic argon plasma coagulation for refractory cases.
learningPoints: Radiation proctitis typically presents months to years after pelvic radiotherapy with rectal bleeding and tenesmus.
Colonoscopy in radiation proctitis shows characteristic telangiectasia in the rectum.
Common malignancies requiring pelvic radiotherapy include prostate, cervical, and rectal cancer.
references: Dalsania RM, Shah KP, Stotsky-Himelfarb E, Hoffe S, Willingham FF. Management of Long-Term Toxicity From Pelvic Radiation Therapy. Am Soc Clin Oncol Educ Book. 2021 Apr;(41):147–57.
